## Binary Search Tree Insertion — Time Complexity

**Average case:** O(log n) — each comparison eliminates roughly half the remaining tree.

**Worst case:** O(n) — occurs when insertions arrive in sorted order, degenerating the tree into a linked list.

### BST Insert Pseudocode

```
function bst_insert(root, key):
    if root is null:
        return new Node(key)
    if key < root.key:
        root.left = bst_insert(root.left, key)
    else if key > root.key:
        root.right = bst_insert(root.right, key)
    return root
```

---

## Red-Black Tree — How It Maintains Balance

A red-black tree is a self-balancing BST that enforces five invariants:

| # | Invariant |
|---|-----------|
| 1 | Every node is either **red** or **black** |
| 2 | The **root** is always **black** |
| 3 | Every **null leaf** (NIL) is **black** |
| 4 | A **red** node cannot have a **red** child (no two consecutive reds) |
| 5 | Every path from a node to its descendant NIL leaves has the **same number of black nodes** (black-height) |

These invariants guarantee that the longest path is at most **2× the shortest path**, bounding the height to **O(2 log(n+1))** → **O(log n)**.

### Insertion Strategy

1. **Insert** the node as in a standard BST, color it **red**.
2. **Fix violations** by walking up the tree, applying one of three cases:

### Fix-Up Pseudocode

```
function rb_insert_fixup(tree, z):
    while z.parent.color == RED:
        if z.parent == z.parent.parent.left:
            uncle = z.parent.parent.right

            // Case 1: Uncle is RED → recolor
            if uncle.color == RED:
                z.parent.color = BLACK
                uncle.color = BLACK
                z.parent.parent.color = RED
                z = z.parent.parent          // move up

            else:
                // Case 2: z is a right child → left rotate to reduce to Case 3
                if z == z.parent.right:
                    z = z.parent
                    left_rotate(tree, z)

                // Case 3: z is a left child → recolor + right rotate
                z.parent.color = BLACK
                z.parent.parent.color = RED
                right_rotate(tree, z.parent.parent)

        else:
            // Mirror: swap left/right in all of the above
            ...

    tree.root.color = BLACK                  // enforce invariant 2
```

### Rotation Pseudocode

```
function left_rotate(tree, x):
    y = x.right
    x.right = y.left           // adopt y's left subtree
    if y.left != NIL:
        y.left.parent = x
    y.parent = x.parent        // y takes x's position
    if x.parent == NIL:
        tree.root = y
    else if x == x.parent.left:
        x.parent.left = y
    else:
        x.parent.right = y
    y.left = x                 // x becomes y's left child
    x.parent = y
```

`right_rotate` is the symmetric mirror.

### Why It Works

| Operation | Recolor | Rotate | Net effect |
|-----------|---------|--------|------------|
| **Case 1** | O(1) | 0 | Moves violation up 2 levels — may repeat O(log n) times |
| **Case 2** | 0 | 1 | Converts to Case 3 |
| **Case 3** | O(1) | 1 | **Terminates** the fix-up |

**Worst case:** O(log n) recolorings + at most **2 rotations** per insertion.

**Insertion complexity: O(log n) guaranteed** — the tree never degenerates.

---

### TL;DR

| Tree | Insert (avg) | Insert (worst) | Guarantee |
|------|-------------|----------------|-----------|
| BST | O(log n) | O(n) | None |
| Red-Black | O(log n) | O(log n) | ≤ 2 rotations per insert, height ≤ 2·log(n+1) |
